rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is established by the parent statement: "two strategic adaptation interventions... to stabilize future water supply-demand equilibrium under climate change... ("..." leakage reduction)." The policy/verbatim text specify leakage-reduction measures: "Implement leak control measures including Active Leakage Control, Pressure Management, Network Rehabilitation, and improved speed and quality of repair" and "Leak control... Active Leakage Control, Pressure Management, Networks Rehabilitations and Speed & Quality of Repair." These actions directly reduce water loss in distribution systems (resource efficiency in water use), so the primary category Resource Efficiency is appropriate despite missing explicit outcome tokens.
policy_statement: Implement leak control measures including Active Leakage Control, Pressure Management, Network Rehabilitation, and improved speed and quality of repair.
mechanism_description: Implementing leak detection, pressure management, network rehabilitation, and faster higher-quality repairs reduces leakage in the water distribution network and lowers overall water losses.
primary_causal_pathway: Same water service → ↓ water losses → ↓ water production/pumping/treatment energy → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Utilities improve distribution system performance via leak detection, pressure management, pipe/network rehabilitation, and faster/higher-quality repairs. (2) Process: Lower physical leakage reduces non-revenue water, so less raw water must be abstracted and less treated water must be pumped and processed to meet demand; pressure optimization also reduces pumping set. (3) Outcome: Reduced electricity/fuel use across the water supply chain leads to lower associated GHG emissions (and often reduced upstream water withdrawals).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced energy demand for water extraction, treatment, and pumping due to delivering the same water service with less production, which lowers GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: reducing distribution leaks lowers the amount of water that must be produced and conveyed for the same delivered service, cutting energy use and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the outcome is ultimately mitigation (lower emissions), the mechanism operates first and foremost through efficiency—reducing resource/energy inputs per unit of service—so “Resource Efficiency” best matches the dominant pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: in gravity-fed, low-energy systems the mitigation benefit may be small and the main benefit could be water security/adaptation; however, the canonical mechanism still describes efficiency via loss reduction, so the primary category remains Resource Efficiency.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy that reduces distribution leakage/water losses follows the same core pathway of reduced required production/handling for the same service, regardless of technology (pressure management, rehab, detection, repairs).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: whether quantified as mitigation, drought resilience, or utility modernization depends on local energy mix, pumping intensity, and baseline leakage and should be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the water system is largely gravity-fed and treatment/pumping energy is minimal, climate mitigation impacts may be marginal and the policy may function more as water-security/adaptation; conversely, where water is desalinated or heavily pumped, mitigation benefits can be very large. In some cases reduced leaks can increase consumption (“rebound”) if saved water is used to expand service, partially offsetting energy/GHG gains.
key_indicators: "Leak control"; "Active Leakage Control"; "Pressure Management"; "Networks Rehabilitations"; "Speed & Quality of Repair"
co_benefits: Improved water security/reliability; reduced non-revenue water and operating costs; reduced abstraction/withdrawals.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning/strategy or a utility performance program depending on whether the full policy includes targets/requirements; the provided text lists measures only, so classified as infrastructure/operations investment.
